essence: Logistics is an asset + network business moving goods - economics driven by density, utilisation (load factors), and network scale.
Often thin-margin and fragmented; profit comes from scale, asset utilisation, route/network density, and value-added services (3PL, warehousing).
Asset-heavy (fleets, warehouses) vs asset-light (forwarders, tech, 3PL) models have very different economics.
E-commerce, quick-commerce, and supply-chain-resilience trends are massive demand drivers; tech/automation is reshaping cost.
In India, GST + infrastructure (highways, freight corridors) + formalisation are tailwinds structurally lowering logistics cost.

definition.oneLiner: Companies that move, store, and manage the flow of goods - freight, warehousing, last-mile delivery, and supply-chain services.
definition.jobToBeDone: Get goods from where they're made to where they're needed - reliably, fast, and cheaply - and manage the storage and flow in between.

valueChain.profitPools: Profit pools sit in dense, high-utilisation networks, in value-added 3PL/warehousing and integrated supply-chain services, and in express/last-mile at scale; pure commoditised trucking is thin and fragmented; tech platforms capture value by aggregating and optimising.
valueChain.powerShift: Value is shifting from fragmented, asset-heavy trucking -> organised 3PL + tech-enabled networks + warehousing -> data/AI-optimised, integrated supply-chain services, as e-commerce, formalisation (GST), and infrastructure reshape the market.

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Revenue per shipment / per tonne-km (freight); per parcel (express); per sq ft (warehousing)
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Volume x Yield. Volume = shipments/tonnage moved (driven by demand + network); yield = price per unit, lifted by density, value-added services, and utilisation.

costStructure.shape: Dominated by fuel, fleet/vehicle (or lease), labour/drivers, and warehousing real estate; thin margins mean density and utilisation (empty miles/idle space kill economics) are decisive.
costStructure.leverage: Operating leverage from network density and asset utilisation - filling trucks and warehouses spreads fixed cost; sub-scale or low-density operators bleed on empty miles and idle space.

competitionAndMoats.concentration: Highly fragmented (especially trucking), consolidating toward organised, tech-enabled and 3PL players; express/last-mile and warehousing more concentrated among scaled operators.

structuralForces.regulation: Moderate - transport/road regulation, e-way bills & GST, labour, emissions; infrastructure policy (highways, freight corridors, multimodal) is a huge swing factor.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Cyclical - tied to trade, industrial output, and consumption; fuel-price sensitive; e-commerce/quick-commerce add structural growth.
structuralForces.substitution: Modal shifts (road vs rail vs air vs sea), in-housing vs outsourcing (3PL), and (long-term) automation/drones at the margin.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Fuel prices and (for asset-light) carrier capacity swing costs; large shippers/e-commerce platforms wield strong buyer power; driver availability is a constraint.

aiPenetration.maturity: Medium-to-high - routing, network optimisation, and warehouse automation are core AI/tech use cases.
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: AI directly attacks the core cost levers - empty miles, idle space, last-mile cost - via routing, forecasting, and warehouse automation; it also enables freight-matching platforms that aggregate fragmented capacity.
opportunities: Higher utilisation & lower cost per shipment

ranges: EBITDA margin: thin for freight (~mid-single digits); higher for 3PL/warehousing
Load factor: higher = better; empty miles kill economics
India logistics cost: historically ~13-14% of GDP, targeted lower
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

valueLevers: Raise asset utilisation / load factors (cut empty miles)

keyTerms.Load factor / utilisation: How full trucks/warehouses are - the core efficiency metric.
keyTerms.Empty miles: Distance run without cargo - pure cost, no revenue.
keyTerms.Last mile: The final delivery leg to the customer - costly and hard.
keyTerms.Tonne-km: One tonne moved one km - the freight volume unit.
keyTerms.E-way bill: GST-mandated electronic document for goods movement in India.
keyTerms.Dedicated Freight Corridor: Rail freight-only corridors that cut logistics cost.

questionsToAskFirst: Load factor / utilisation and network density?
Mix of commodity freight vs value-added 3PL/warehousing?
rookieTraps: Treating asset-heavy and asset-light models alike
Underrating network density as the moat

whatsUniqueHere: Logistics is a density-and-utilisation business hiding behind thin margins: the difference between a profitable and a loss-making operator is whether the trucks run full and the warehouses stay busy - empty miles and idle space are pure loss. Scale and network density are the real moat (hard to replicate), and in India a once-in-a-generation tailwind - GST, highways, freight corridors, and e-commerce - is formalising a hugely fragmented market and structurally lowering the country's logistics cost.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: A massive, historically fragmented and unorganised market (especially trucking) now formalising fast post-GST; organised players and 3PL (Delhivery, TCI, Mahindra Logistics, Blue Dart, Allcargo) gaining share; e-commerce and quick-commerce driving express/warehousing; government infrastructure (Gati Shakti, freight corridors, National Logistics Policy) targeting lower logistics cost.
regulatorsAndPolicy: National Logistics Policy & PM Gati Shakti
demandDrivers: Formalisation shifting volume to organised players
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

similarTo: Internet & E-commerce - networks, density, thin margins

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India logistics: rapid formalisation post-GST and a strong infrastructure push (Gati Shakti, freight corridors, National Logistics Policy) lowering costs; Delhivery and organised 3PL gaining share; quick-commerce driving dense urban warehousing/dark stores; AI routing and warehouse automation scaling.
